behaviour.mcq: Select one option. Answer revealed immediately on selection.

questions.stem: The single most important prognostic factor in cutaneous melanoma is:
questions.explanation: Breslow thickness (depth in mm from the granular layer to the deepest tumour cell) is the strongest prognostic factor, together with ulceration and mitotic rate. Clark level is now largely historical.

questions.stem: In the ABCDE rule for melanoma, the “E” stands for:
questions.explanation: Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Colour variegation, Diameter >6 mm, Evolution. A changing lesion is one of the most important warning signs.

questions.stem: Actinic (solar) keratosis is best described as:

options.A: A benign lesion with no malignant potential
options.B: A premalignant lesion that may progress to squamous cell carcinoma
options.C: A subtype of basal cell carcinoma

questions.explanation: AK is an intra-epidermal dysplasia driven by cumulative UV exposure; a proportion progress to invasive SCC (part of the SCC-in-situ spectrum). It is considered premalignant and cannot itself metastasise.

questions.stem: Cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma most commonly metastasises via:
questions.explanation: SCC can metastasise — most often first to regional lymph nodes. Risk is higher on lip/ear, in immunosuppressed patients, and with poor differentiation. (BCC, by contrast, almost never metastasises.)

questions.stem: A slowly growing pearly papule with a rolled border and surface telangiectasia on the face is most typical of:
questions.explanation: Nodular BCC: pearly/translucent papule, rolled telangiectatic edge, may ulcerate centrally (“rodent ulcer”). It is the commonest skin cancer — locally invasive but rarely metastasising.

questions.stem: A positive Nikolsky sign is characteristically found in:
questions.explanation: Nikolsky (lateral pressure shears the epidermis) is positive in pemphigus (intra-epidermal acantholysis) and in SJS/TEN. It is negative in bullous pemphigoid, where blisters are sub-epidermal and tense.

questions.stem: The autoantibodies in pemphigus vulgaris are directed against:
questions.explanation: PV = IgG against desmoglein 3 (± dsg1) in desmosomes → intra-epidermal acantholysis, flaccid blisters and painful oral erosions. DIF shows intercellular “chicken-wire” IgG/C3.

questions.stem: Direct immunofluorescence in bullous pemphigoid classically shows:

options.B: Linear IgG/C3 along the basement membrane
options.C: Granular IgA in the dermal papillae

questions.explanation: BP = linear IgG/C3 along the dermo-epidermal junction (targets BP180/BP230 in hemidesmosomes). Presents in the elderly with itchy, tense blisters; mucosa usually spared.

questions.stem: Dermatitis herpetiformis is most strongly associated with:
questions.explanation: DH = intensely pruritic grouped vesicles on extensor surfaces; DIF shows granular IgA in dermal papillae. It is the cutaneous expression of gluten-sensitive enteropathy — treat with a gluten-free diet ± dapsone.

questions.stem: Which nail change is most characteristic of psoriasis and correlates with psoriatic arthritis?
questions.explanation: Psoriatic nails show pitting, onycholysis, “oil-drop” (salmon) patches and subungual hyperkeratosis. Nail involvement is a marker of increased psoriatic arthritis risk.

questions.stem: The Auspitz sign (pinpoint bleeding when scale is removed) is seen in:
questions.explanation: Removing the silvery scale of a psoriatic plaque exposes dilated dermal papillary capillaries → pinpoint bleeding (Auspitz sign). Psoriasis shows the Koebner phenomenon too.

questions.stem: The characteristic sun-exposure–related eruption of systemic lupus erythematosus is:

options.A: A malar (butterfly) rash sparing the nasolabial folds
options.B: A heliotrope rash of the eyelids
options.D: A herald patch followed by a Christmas-tree pattern

questions.explanation: Acute cutaneous lupus = photosensitive malar/butterfly erythema that characteristically spares the nasolabial folds. Heliotrope and Gottron papules belong to dermatomyositis.

questions.stem: Gottron papules and a heliotrope rash are diagnostic clues to:
questions.explanation: Dermatomyositis: violaceous heliotrope eyelid rash and Gottron papules over the MCP/IP joints, plus proximal myopathy. In adults it carries an association with underlying malignancy.

questions.stem: The painless, indurated genital ulcer of primary syphilis is called a:
questions.explanation: Primary syphilis = a solitary, painless, indurated chancre at the inoculation site. (Painful ulcers suggest chancroid/HSV; condylomata lata are moist secondary-stage papules.)

questions.stem: Hutchinson’s triad of congenital syphilis comprises interstitial keratitis, Hutchinson teeth and:
questions.explanation: The triad is interstitial keratitis, notched Hutchinson incisors and eighth-nerve deafness. Neurosyphilis and aortitis are late acquired complications, not part of the triad.

questions.stem: Which is TRUE of scabies?

options.A: The pruritus typically begins within hours of infestation
options.B: Itch and rash reflect a delayed hypersensitivity reaction developing over ~4–6 weeks
options.C: It is caused by a louse

questions.explanation: In a first infestation, symptoms of Sarcoptes scabiei take about 4–6 weeks to appear — a delayed hypersensitivity to the mite. Look for burrows in finger web spaces; treat with topical permethrin.

questions.stem: Crusted (Norwegian) scabies is most typically seen in:
questions.explanation: Crusted scabies = huge mite burden with thick hyperkeratotic crusts, occurring in the immunosuppressed (HIV, elderly, corticosteroids). It is highly contagious and may need oral ivermectin plus topical therapy.

questions.stem: A recognised cutaneous side-effect of potent topical corticosteroids is:

options.A: Skin atrophy with striae and telangiectasia

questions.explanation: Prolonged potent topical steroids cause atrophy, striae, telangiectasia, perioral dermatitis, tachyphylaxis and (on the face) a steroid-rosacea/acneiform eruption.

questions.stem: Isotretinoin for severe acne requires strict precautions mainly because it is:

options.A: Hepatotoxic and requires weekly liver biopsy
options.B: A potent teratogen requiring reliable contraception

questions.explanation: Oral isotretinoin is highly teratogenic — pregnancy prevention is mandatory. Monitor lipids and liver enzymes; other effects include cheilitis, dryness, myalgia and mood changes.

questions.stem: Which factor is NOT part of the pathogenesis of acne vulgaris?
questions.explanation: Acne pathogenesis = follicular hyperkeratinisation, androgen-driven sebum, C. acnes colonisation and inflammation. Anti-desmoglein antibodies belong to pemphigus, not acne.

questions.stem: A feature that distinguishes rosacea from acne vulgaris is:

options.B: Absence of comedones with a background of flushing/telangiectasia

questions.explanation: Rosacea has no comedones (unlike acne) and features central-face flushing, erythema, telangiectasia, papules/pustules and (late) rhinophyma. Ocular involvement is common.

questions.stem: Erythema nodosum is best characterised as:

options.A: A septal panniculitis presenting as tender red nodules on the shins
options.B: A vesicular eruption of the trunk

questions.explanation: EN is a septal panniculitis — tender erythematous nodules on the shins that resolve like a bruise without ulceration. Triggers: streptococcal infection, sarcoidosis, IBD, TB, drugs (e.g. sulfonamides, OCP).

questions.stem: The commonest malignant tumour of the skin overall is:
questions.explanation: Basal cell carcinoma is the most common skin cancer (and the most common human cancer). SCC is second; melanoma is far less common but causes most skin-cancer deaths.

questions.stem: Which is a recognised risk factor for melanoma?

options.B: A high number of atypical (dysplastic) naevi

questions.explanation: Melanoma risk rises with numerous / atypical naevi, fair skin, light eyes, red-blond hair, family/personal history and intermittent blistering sunburn. Dark eyes and darker phototypes are relatively protective.

questions.stem: The autoantibody target in bullous pemphigoid is a component of the:
questions.explanation: BP antibodies target the hemidesmosome proteins BP180 (collagen XVII) and BP230, producing a sub-epidermal split — hence tense blisters and a negative Nikolsky sign.

questions.stem: First-line drug treatment for acute urticaria is:
questions.explanation: Urticaria (transient wheals lasting <24 h, histamine-mediated) is treated first with non-sedating H1 antihistamines, up-dosed as needed. Watch for associated angio-oedema.

questions.stem: Which statement about basal cell carcinoma is correct?

options.B: It is locally invasive but very rarely metastasises

questions.explanation: BCC arises from basal keratinocytes, is locally destructive but almost never metastasises. Main risk factor is chronic UV exposure; commonest on the head and neck.

questions.stem: A patient develops widespread flaccid blisters and painful mouth erosions; biopsy shows intra-epidermal acantholysis. The most likely diagnosis is:
questions.explanation: Intra-epidermal split with acantholysis, flaccid blisters and prominent mucosal involvement = pemphigus vulgaris. Untreated it can be life-threatening; needs systemic immunosuppression.

questions.stem: Which laboratory test is used to monitor treatment response / activity in syphilis?
questions.explanation: Non-treponemal tests (VDRL/RPR) are quantitative and fall with successful treatment, so they are used to monitor activity. Treponemal tests (TPHA, FTA-ABS) usually stay positive for life.

questions.stem: Dupilumab, increasingly used in severe atopic dermatitis, is a monoclonal antibody that blocks:

options.B: The shared IL-4/IL-13 receptor α subunit

questions.explanation: Dupilumab targets IL-4Rα, blocking IL-4 and IL-13 signalling (the Th2 axis central to atopic dermatitis). It has transformed treatment of moderate-to-severe eczema and is also used in asthma.
